comment.body:
I don't know them, I'm researching, thank you. But are they specialized in quantitative trading? I had a professor in college who told me that if he knew how to apply it to the financial markets, he wouldn't be a professor.
That discouraged me because I went into statistics with the goal of working in the financial markets.
I finally came up with a model for sports betting. But they closed my account because it was too profitable. (they are the counterparties themselves and are not interested in winners).
And now I'm working with models here, I don't even know if any of them specialize in quant trading

comment.body:
prof Mateus does it.
but quant math is not exact field. it is very young, there are results and researches but one really needs creativity.
but first you need to know and understand academic level math, and read a lot, experiment a lot, in order to be creative. 
in a 50-80 yearsm there will be a field like quant math, officially, but not now.
it is as developped as manual therapy in medical science.

mail them with confidence :)

Yes, that's precisely why I sometimes feel like I'm studying something that isn't clearly defined. If I had studied the same number of hours in another field that depended more on me, I would feel better about it.
But after so many hours in this field over the years, I feel like there is no clear curriculum...
I don't know if I'm explaining myself well.
Thank you very much for your response.

comment.body: I learned the hard way that, one had better not expect any field of math to have just certain & clearly defined curriculum. comment.body:
@Alma There’s this debate between frequentists and bayesians in statistics, I’m wondering why there’s such a debate in the first place and if you side with one or the other or take a more hybrid approach in that realm? 

Also, which is more applicable to stochastics? It seems like Bayesian bc of its basis in distributions not fixed unknowns but would like to confirm with you
comment.raw_body:
${1} There’s this debate between frequentists and bayesians in statistics, I’m wondering why there’s such a debate in the first place and if you side with one or the other or take a more hybrid approach in that realm? 

Also, which is more applicable to stochastics? It seems like Bayesian bc of its basis in distributions not fixed unknowns but would like to confirm with you

user.bio: Just trying to figure out the puzzle of financial markets

comment.body: My understanding is that Bayesian views probability/uncertainty as a result of insufficient information, whereas frequentist treats uncertainty as default. In reality, I find it's highly debatable...
comment.raw_body: My understanding is that Bayesian views probability/uncertainty as a result of insufficient information, whereas frequentist treats uncertainty as default. In reality, I find it's highly debatable...
